---
title: "6.1 Testing and TDD"
---

## Explanation

A test checks expected behavior. Test-driven development means stating the expected behavior before writing or changing the implementation. The important habit is not the ritual, but the discipline of making expectations explicit.

In this page, the tests are shown immediately after the function. This is a teaching layout: it makes the specification visible next to the implementation while the example is small.

```julia
using Test

function mean_value(xs)
    if isempty(xs)
        error("mean_value is undefined for empty input")
    end

    total = 0.0
    for x in xs
        total += x
    end
    return total / length(xs)
end

@testset "mean_value" begin
    @test mean_value([2.0, 4.0, 6.0]) == 4.0
    @test mean_value([5.0]) == 5.0
    @test mean_value([-1.0, 1.0]) == 0.0
    @test_throws ErrorException mean_value(Float64[])
end
```

The tests are part of the specification. They say what the function should do for normal inputs, one-element inputs, negative values, and empty input.

Do not treat this layout as the recommended structure for real project code. In a real project, separate test code from runtime code, for example by using the project's test directory or test suite. Normal program execution should not run the full test suite, because that can make ordinary calculations unnecessarily slow. Project organization is discussed in [10.2 Project structure, environment, and reproducibility](../reproducible-workflow/project-structure-and-reproducibility.qmd).

When an AI agent writes or changes code, ask it to update the appropriate test files and tell you which command runs the tests.

For floating-point results that are not exactly representable, use approximate comparison:

```julia
@test isapprox(sqrt(2)^2, 2.0)
```

A bad test copies the implementation. A good test checks behavior that you can justify independently, for example by hand calculation or a known result.

## Things to look up

- Unit test
- Regression test
- Test-driven development
- Reference implementation
- Julia `Test` standard library
- `@testset`
- `@test_throws`
- `isapprox`

## Exercise

Write a Julia function `mean_value(xs)`. For this teaching exercise, place a `@testset` immediately after it so that the function and specification can be read together. Include at least five tests. For each test, write one sentence explaining what property it checks.

## Notes for the exercise

- Include empty input or explain why it is invalid.
- Include a one-element input.
- Include repeated values.
- Include negative values.
- Include a case whose answer is known by hand.
- Use `isapprox` when exact floating-point equality is not appropriate.
- Tests should check the specification, not copy the implementation.
- In real project files, separate tests from runtime code and run them through the project test command.

## Optional coding check

Run the file with Julia. If a test fails, read the failure message before changing either the function or the test.
